text: Hi, I'm Maheen from AWS.
Hi, I'm Hamza from Sales Flow and this is my architecture.
So today we are talking about a serverless architecture that helps with building and
customizing workflows for large organizations. So Hamza, talk to me about the customer challenge
that led to the solution. Sure. For businesses spread over large
geographical landscapes, effective compliance control, monitoring, and visibility is crucial
for growth. For example, consumer good companies hire hundreds or thousands of field forces
to improve product visibility in stores and any delays, for example, in replacement of stocks
can lead to losses. This is where Sales Flow Engage comes in. It helps to effectively manage
on-ground operations by providing live visibility into any tasks that are performed and actually,
gives insight that help empower informed decisions. Let's dive in.
Sure. So there are two parts of the application. First is the web application that is used to
configure workflows and journey plans. So let's dive straight into that. So the domain is hosted on
to Amazon Route 53 and then we have API Gateway that serves as the communication between the front
and the backend and the backend of the application. API Gateway then invokes the backend processing
through AWS Fargate and Lambda. So we use AWS Fargate for more complex and long running jobs such as data
validations and image processing. And what about jobs that don't take long? So we use AWS Lambda for short running
jobs such as transactional data processing. It could be add, update, delete of operations of the web application itself.
And it is actually connected through Amazon RDS to store the transactional data. We also have Elastic Cache
for frequently accessed data so that we don't go on to throttle the database.
So what sort of volumes are we talking when you use Elastic Cache? Absolutely. So we have observed that
at peak load times it can be a million concurrent users. Hence, it makes a lot of sense to use it for
frequently accessed data such as session management and user permissions so that we don't end up throttling
the transactions onto the database. Now, coming to the mobile application there, help me understand
how does a user get access to the data? Absolutely. So the mobile application is sending
large streams of data such as the workflow analytics data and the user tracking data.
So we reliably ingest that data using Kinesis Firehose and Firehose actually natively backs up that data
onto an S3 bucket. We also have AWS Lambda. So what happens is the Kinesis actually then aggregates that
data and sends the payload onto a Lambda function. The Lambda function passes that data and passes onto a
state machine that is configured using AWS step functions. So whatever workflow that was designed
on the web application is actually modularly configured at the backend through AWS step functions. So these
configurations could be something as like alarms for the users for key business metrics. So whenever the
thresholds for those metrics are breached, the step function notifies the simple notification service
to prompt the user to take some actions. So these prompts could be through email or SMS or from the
mobile application as well. Okay, fantastic. And you've got dashboard and reporting for the web application
there and there is Athena and Glue. So help me understand the purpose for Athena and Glue here.
Absolutely. So the data is also then sent to Lambda which is stored onto a relational database for safekeeping.
And then actually what Kinesis does very interesting is to send the data in a consolidated manner into
Amazon S3. So let me just go and draw a line here. Yeah.
And what it does interesting is to actually convert the data into Apache Parky format which is a big data
ready format and it actually compresses the data by 90 percent. This helps us to actually make analysis
a little bit more of a data that we can do. So we can do a data that we can do a data that we can do a data
for data lake. And we use Amazon Glue to actually do Scrawler actually to identify, classify that data and then to
catalog it. Then we use Amazon Athena to actually query that data on demand. What happens is when the application
user wants dashboards or reporting it can go and query Amazon Athena and it can get the data on demand from
S3 without the need of actually provisioning long running EMR clusters or warehouses.
Fantastic. So Hamza, obviously using dashboards and reporting one of the main activities is efficiency gains.
So what stats can you quote in terms of what the customers have been able to achieve?
I think with enhanced visibility of data and reporting, customers are actually able to improve
their field force performance by 30 percent, primarily focusing on two key metrics that is
the in-field time spent and better market coverage.
Fantastic. Hamza, thank you for walking us through the fantastic architecture you've built here for
workflow management and thank you for watching this is my architecture. Thank you.
